text: Here we present a case of a forty-eight-year-old male with invasive rectal adenocarcinoma, stage T-N-N-zero, who received neoadjuvant chemoradiation and subsequently underwent low anterior resection and transanal total mesorectal excision of the rectum with hand-sewn colloanal anastomosis and diverting loop ileostomy. Four months after his index procedure, the patient presented with prolapse of his neorectum of approximately three to four centimeters, leading to drainage and discomfort.
The patient is positioned supine on the operating table, prepped and draped in sterile fashion, as the surgical robot is docked for the planned neorectopexy procedure.
The surgeon performs extensive circumferential dissection to free pelvic adhesions surrounding the neorectum, carefully separating the neorectum from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon mobilizes the neorectum to reduce the prolapse into the abdominal cavity without tension. A tuovicral stitch is placed in the serosa of the neorectum and sutured to the anterior sacral ligament.

The surgeon performs additional dissection to ensure tension-free reduction of the neorectum. A 2-0 V-lock stitch is placed in the serosa and sutured to the anterior sacral ligament for added support, and the suture is tied to complete the neorectopexy.
The repair was inspected and hemostasis achieved. The patient's post-operative course was uneventful and was discharged home on post-operative day one.
At three-month follow-up, the patient was symptom-free, having normal bowel function, and underwent elective reversal of his prior ileostomy.
